perturbation.original_text: Each post shall be created by PAPA JOHN'S, but subject to ABG's Approval in each instance. PAPA JOHN'S shall provide ABG and CELEBRITY with all suitable and necessary language, compliant with all Federal Trade Commission ("FTC") standards and requirements to include in each post (each a or the, "Required Disclosure") which shall clearly and conspicuously disclose to the public that ABG and/or CELEBRITY has been paid by PAPA JOHN'S in connection with such posts.
perturbation.changed_text: Each post shall be created by PAPA JOHN'S, subject to ABG's suggestions, not Approval, in each instance. PAPA JOHN'S shall provide ABG and CELEBRITY with language that may or may not be compliant with all Federal Trade Commission ("FTC") standards and requirements to include in each post (each a or the, "Suggested Disclosure") which does not have to clearly and conspicuously disclose to the public that ABG and/or CELEBRITY has been paid by PAPA JOHN'S in connection with such posts.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "Approval" to "suggestions" and stating the disclosures do not need to be clear contradicts FTC regulations regarding endorsements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The modification now permits endorsements that lack clear and conspicuous disclosure of the paid relationship, potentially misleading consumers.
perturbation.law_explanation: This regulation requires that endorsements disclose material connections between the endorser and the seller of the product, which this modification attempts to undermine. The regulation states: 'When there exists a connection between the endorser and the seller of the advertised product that might materially affect the weight or credibility of the endorsement (i.e., the connection is not reasonably expected by the audience), such connection must be fully disclosed.' By making the disclosure optional and suggesting compliance with FTC standards is not necessary, it directly conflicts with this regulation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Federal Register :: Request Access Request Access Request Access Due to aggressive automated scraping of FederalRegister.gov and eCFR.gov, programmatic access to these sites is limited to access to our extensive developer APIs. If you are human user receiving this message, we can add your IP address to a set of IPs that can access FederalRegister.gov & eCFR.gov; complete the CAPTCHA (bot test) below and click "Request Access". This process will be necessary for each IP address you wish to access the site from, requests are valid for approximately one quarter (three months) after which the process may need to be repeated. Request Access for 72.74.68.69 An official website of the United States government. If you want to request a wider IP range, first request access for your current IP, and then use the "Site Feedback" button found in the lower left-hand side to make the request.
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts FTC regulations regarding endorsements by changing 'Approval' to 'suggestions' and stating the disclosures do not need to be clear. The Law Explanation states that the regulation requires disclosure of connections between the endorser and the seller, and this modification attempts to undermine that. The original contract text required 'Required Disclosure' to be compliant with FTC standards, while the changed text introduces a 'Suggested Disclosure' that 'does not have to clearly and conspicuously disclose' the payment, directly conflicting with the disclosure requirements outlined in the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: The failure at any time of any party to demand strict performance of another party of any of the terms, covenants or conditions set forth in this Agreement will not be construed as a continuing waiver or relinquishment thereof, and any party may, at any time, demand strict and complete performance of any other party of such terms, covenants, and conditions.
perturbation.changed_text: The failure at any time of any party to demand strict performance of another party of any of the terms, covenants or conditions set forth in this Agreement will be construed as a continuing waiver or relinquishment thereof, and any party may not, at any time, demand strict and complete performance of any other party of such terms, covenants, and conditions.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that failure to demand strict performance is a continuing waiver, it contradicts established contract law principles regarding the prevention of unintentional waiver.
perturbation.contradicted_law: This directly opposes the general principle of contract law that waivers must be explicit and intentional.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 1-308, although primarily related to negotiable instruments, embodies the broader legal principle that a party's rights are not waived simply by failing to demand them immediately. The statute and its interpretation assert that an explicit reservation of rights prevents waiver. This modified clause implies the opposite, stating a passive failure to demand is a waiver.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-308. Performance or Acceptance Under Reservation of Rights. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-308. Performance or Acceptance Under Reservation of Rights. (a) A party that with explicit reservation of rights performs or promises performance or assents to performance in a manner demanded or offered by the other party does not thereby prejudice the rights reserved. Such words as "without prejudice," "under protest," or the like are sufficient. (b) Subsection (a) does not apply to an accord and satisfaction. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the principle outlined in UCC § 1-308, as described in the Law Explanation. The original text preserves the right to demand strict performance, even if it wasn't initially enforced. The changed text states that failure to demand strict performance is a waiver, which undermines the right to demand it later. Snippet 1, referring to UCC § 1-308, states that a party doesn't prejudice their rights if they explicitly reserve them, even when performing under demand. The changed text creates the opposite scenario, implying a waiver even without explicit reservation, which constitutes a direct contradiction and violates the legal principle preventing unintentional waiver.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, without regard to its principles of conflicts of law.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, with explicit and required regard to its principles of conflicts of law such that any laws or statutes outside of Delaware can be used for interpretation.
perturbation.explanation: The added phrase now mandates that the principles of conflicts of law must be used such that outside laws can be used for interpretation, which directly contradicts the intention of specifying a single jurisdiction (Delaware) for governing the contract. Conflict of law principles are supposed to determine which state's laws to use; the modification allows outside laws to be forced into the interpretation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: This attempts to use conflict of law principles to allow other state laws to be used to interpret the contract in Delaware. The purpose of a choice-of-law clause is to simplify disputes by naming which state's laws will be used. 28 U.S. Code § 1391 dictates venue.
perturbation.law_explanation: While 28 U.S. Code § 1391 deals with venue and not directly with the choice of law, the spirit of the statute reflects an intent to provide clarity and predictability in jurisdictional matters. By creating ambiguity in which laws apply (Delaware + potentially other state laws due to conflict of law principles), the modification introduces uncertainty and potential legal challenges, undermining the goal of a clear governing law provision and increasing the likelihood of disputes over venue and applicable law.

Venue generally (a) Applicability of Section .-Except as otherwise provided by law- (1) this section shall govern the venue of all civil actions brought in district courts of the United States; and (2) the proper venue for a civil action shall be determined without regard to whether the action is local or transitory in nature. (b) Venue in General .-A civil action may be brought in- (1) a judicial district in which a
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies that the agreement is governed by the laws of Delaware, without regard to its principles of conflicts of law. The changed text mandates that the principles of conflicts of law must be used, such that outside laws can be used for interpretation. This introduces ambiguity and potential legal challenges, undermining the goal of a clear governing law provision, as the intent of the original text was to avoid the application of other state's laws. The introduction of conflict of law principles allows for laws outside of Delaware to be used for interpretation, which directly contradicts the intent of specifying a single jurisdiction. As described in the Perturbation Explanation, conflict of law principles are supposed to determine which state's laws to use; the modification allows outside laws to be forced into the interpretation.
